mnemonics.title: Four Delays in Packet Switching
mnemonics.content: NTPQ: N(odal) T(ransmission) P(ropagation) Q(ueuing) - Remember as 'Not To Push Quickly'

acronyms.usage: Maximum packet size that can be transmitted over a network segment without fragmentation
Mechanisms to guarantee performance parameters for specific traffic types
Identifier used in virtual circuit packet switching to label packets belonging to a connection
Error detection code in trailer for detecting transmission errors
Field in IP header used for QoS to indicate priority and service requirements

visualTips.visualization: Draw a rectangle divided into three sections: Header (left, ~20-60 bytes) with address/control fields, Payload (middle, variable size) as the main data area, and Trailer (right, ~4 bytes) with error checking
Draw a series of 3 routers in a path. Show packet entering Router 1, pausing briefly (stored), then moving to Router 2, and so on. Each stop represents storing and checking before forwarding.
Draw a router with input queues. Show packets waiting with different wait times by drawing them at different positions in the queue, illustrating why jitter occurs.

Draw two scenarios: Virtual Circuit as a numbered highway with all trucks (packets) following the same pre-marked path; Datagram as a city with each delivery truck choosing its own route independently.

analogies.analogy: Like sending a book through separate postcards - each page (packet) can take different mail routes, arrive out of order, and must be reassembled by the recipient.
Like a relay race where each runner (router) must fully receive the baton (packet), verify they have a good grip, then run their segment to the next runner.
Imagine ordering 10 pizzas for delivery. If one arrives in 20 minutes, another in 45 minutes, another in 25 minutes - the inconsistent arrival times are like jitter.
Like a mailbox that's full - any new letters (packets) get returned or thrown away until someone empties the mailbox (buffer clears).
